item.title: MCSA 70-410 Lab #5 - Working with Storage
item.description:
We’re going to have some fun with storage today!

It’s come to my attention that my version of the 70-410 may have been much heavier on PowerShell than others. I’m mostly going to focus on the PowerShell, still, but I encourage you to know how to do these tasks in the GUI. Especially when it comes to Storage and Hyper-V.

Initial Setup

Go ahead and spin up a storage server. Let’s call this Stor1.

.\New-ChildVM.ps1 Stor1

Go ahead and add at least three more drives to the machine. This lab definitely had lots of room to play around with.

$vm = get-vm Stor1

# Foreach 1..3 Create a new dynamic VHD. % is short for Foreach-Item
1..3 | % {New-VHD -Dynamic -SizeBytes 100GB -Path E:\Lab\VM\Stor1-$_.vhdx}

# And add each disk to the machine
1..3 | % {$VM | Add-VMHardDiskDrive -Path E:\Lab\VM\Stor1-$_.vhdx }

The settings for this server should look something like this now:

And you can verify through PowerShell:

PS E:\Lab\scripts> $vm | Get-VMHardDiskDrive

VMName ControllerType ControllerNumber ControllerLocation DiskNumber Path
------ -------------- ---------------- ------------------ ---------- ----
Stor1  SCSI           0                0                             E:\Lab\VM\Stor1.vhdx
Stor1  SCSI           0                1                             E:\Lab\VM\Stor1-1.vhdx
Stor1  SCSI           0                2                             E:\Lab\VM\Stor1-2.vhdx
Stor1  SCSI           0                3                             E:\Lab\VM\Stor1-3.vhdx

At this point, go ahead and go through all of the initial setup. IP, DNS, Domain, Time, Name, Etc. Review the previous two lessons if needed. Come back when you’re done.

Here is where Util1 really comes in handy. Close the windows for Stor1 and open Util1. Click on Manage > Add servers and add Stor1 (and any other servers).

Now you can manage all of the installed roles right from one server. Even better, if you right click a server, you get the option to open a PowerShell connection to that computer! Try it.

You can also manually run PowerShell and issue this command (hint: know this).

Enter-PSSession Stor1

How easy is that? You can see now that we will never have to touch Stor1 (or the others) again. We’ll do everything from this server.

Still in server manager, let’s go ahead and install all the roles we need on Stor1. Click Manage > Add Roles and Features. Install at least the following features on Stor1 (I don’t know what all we’re going to do just yet).

You can install individual roles on another server from PowerShell like this:

Install-WindowsFeature -ComputerName Stor1 -Name Files-Services, FS-Data-Deduplication [etc...] -IncludeManagementTools

Storage Pools

If you drill down to the disks in File and Storage Services in Server Manager, you’ll see that the 3 disks we added are offline. That’s okay. Take a look at storage pools. You’ll see the “primordial pool”, which means you haven’t created one. Let’s create one.

We’re going to do this through PowerShell, but I strongly recommend you back and do this in the GUI.

# Get the disks that can be pooled (not the one with C:\)
$PhysicalDisks = (Get-PhysicalDisk -CanPool $True)

New-StoragePool -FriendlyName Pool1 -PhysicalDisks $PhysicalDisks -StorageSubsystemFriendlyName "Storage Spaces*"

For more information about this command, look here.

If you look back in server manager, you now have a new pool under “Storage Spaces”.

We need to add virtual disks to the storage pool in order for it to be any use to us.

New-VirtualDisk -StoragePoolFriendlyName Pool1 -FriendlyName VD1 -Size 100GB -ResiliencySettingName Parity -ProvisioningType Thin

Great! We have a new virtual disk. This one was created with Parity resiliency. You can also choose stripe (no resiliency) and mirror.

Now all that’s left is to bring it online and format it. You can (and should) know how to do this through DiskPart, but you should also do it through Server Manager and Disk Manager.

Now is a good time to create some more virtual disks then pull disconnect a VHD or two from the server and see what happens.

I just removed one of the drives from Stor1. It’s complaining, but my parity disk is still online.

If you add the disk back in, the storage pool should go back to normal.

Next time, we’re going to go over file shares.

item.title: MCSA 70-410 Lab #4 - Install a GUI Utility Server
item.description:
For the exam, it’s important to know how to convert between Core, Minimal Installation, and Full installation. We’re going to do some of that today. Our first subject will be a utility server. We can install all of the management tools on this one to manage the other servers.

The cool thing is that once we get this server running, we won’t need to start console sessions to any server we have already set up.

Looking back on the other lessons, complete the following steps. Try to do as much as possible from memory.

  • Create a server named Util1
  • Rename
  • Set timezone
  • Set a static IP
  • Set the DNS servers, pointing to both DC1 and DC2
  • Reboot

We haven’t gone over how to join the domain yet when you aren’t promoting to a DC. There are two main ways, the old and the new (PowerShell) way. I’m going with the new one.

# Method 1:
netdom join Util1 /domain:Home.local /userd:administrator /passwordd:**********


# Method 2:
Add-Computer -ComputerName Util1 -DomainName Home.local

# You can also specify the -NewName parameter to rename the computer and join the domain in one go.

If you have problems with this, especially if it mentions that the domain could not be contacted, remember that any problem you have is DNS. It’s always DNS.

If you have an issue, start with nslookup and ping, then go from there.

Convert Server Core to Full Install

Like I said before, this is one you will definitely be quizzed on.

Server core is really small and it doesn’t store the GUI binaries “just in case”. That means we need to provide them from the DVD. Mount that ISO again.

Get-VM Util1 | Add-VMDvdDrive -Path E:\Lab\iso\2012R2.ISO

It probably mounted on D:\, but you can check. 

Get-PSDrive

There is an install.wim file located at D:\sources\install.wim that has all of the information we need. Most Windows install disks will have a couple of editions (like Standard and Datacenter) in two flavors (Core and Full). We need to know which edition we’re running.

Get-WindowsEdition -Online

Edition : ServerDatacenter
# So I'm running Datacenter edition

And now we need to know the index of Datacenter edition in the install.wim file. 

Get-WindowsImage -ImagePath D:\sources\install.wim


ImageIndex       : 1
ImageName        : Windows Server 2012 R2 SERVERSTANDARDCORE
ImageDescription : Windows Server 2012 R2 SERVERSTANDARDCORE
ImageSize        : 7,460,086,466 bytes

ImageIndex       : 2
ImageName        : Windows Server 2012 R2 SERVERSTANDARD
ImageDescription : Windows Server 2012 R2 SERVERSTANDARD
ImageSize        : 13,068,096,479 bytes

ImageIndex       : 3
ImageName        : Windows Server 2012 R2 SERVERDATACENTERCORE
ImageDescription : Windows Server 2012 R2 SERVERDATACENTERCORE
ImageSize        : 7,457,614,120 bytes

ImageIndex       : 4
ImageName        : Windows Server 2012 R2 SERVERDATACENTER
ImageDescription : Windows Server 2012 R2 SERVERDATACENTER
ImageSize        : 13,067,663,813 bytes
So I need to target Index4, Datacenter full.

Install-WindowsFeature Server-Gui-Mgmt-Infra, Server-Gui-Shell -Restart -Source wim:D:\sources\install.wim:4
# The :4 at the end is the index

If the server was originally a GUI server and you were converting back, you would only have to run the second command and leave off the -Source parameter.

It will take a while, but once the server reboots you’re running in full GUI mode. Make sure to sign in as Home\Administrator.

Add both DCs to server manager and take a look. Our domain is coming along.

Finally, install the management tools. It’s simple.

Install-WindowsFeature RSAT -IncludeAllSubFeature

This installs all available management tools so that we can manage any other server from this one. Try it! You can connect to AD, DNS, etc.

That’s it for today. Next time we’ll… I’m not sure yet. But we have plenty to do!

MCSA 70-10 Lab #5

item.title: MCSA 70-410 Lab #3 - Creating Child VMs
item.description:
Last time, we made sure we could very easily create chile VMs. This time, we’re going to start setting up servers and create a Forest and Domain.

Also, a quick reminder: please don’t copy and paste all of these commands. You won’t learn them like you should if you do.

Let’s start with our first DC:

cd E:\Lab\scripts\
.\New-ChildVM.ps1 "DC1"

Go ahead and sign in. Remeber how we did a Server Core install? Well, that’s why you don’t have a desktop. This is going to be fun.

First things first. Most of this can be done through sconfig, but we also want to know how to do it manually.

Fix the time / timezone.

timedate.cpl

Rename the server to DC1

powershell #remember, you start in a cmd window
Rename-Computer DC1

#Also an acceptable answer:
netdom renamecomputer %computername% /newname:DC1

Go ahead and run sconfig. It has some interesting options. Might as well turn on Remote Desktop while you’re in there.

Note to self: this would be a good security policy to set.

Go ahead and restart the server, either using sconfig or shutdown -r -t 0.

Install Active Directory

All right, it’s show time. Drop yourself into PowerShell on the server and run this.

Install-WindowsFeature -name AD-Domain-Services -IncludeManagementTools -Restart

Now that the AD feature is installed, we can make a new Forest and Domain. I’m going to use Home.local as my domain name.

In a production environment, best practices state to make your active directory domain a subdomain of public domain that you own, like ad.myawesomebusiness.com. CA’s will no longer sign certificates for fake domains (like *.local). You can, however, get a CA to sign a certificate for exchangeserver.ad.myawesomebusiness.com.

Install-ADDSForest -DomainName Home.local -InstallDNS:$true

This will create a new Forst with a domain name Home.local and it will install a DNS server (which is crucial for AD to function).

You will be asked for a Safe Mode password. Just make it one you’ll remember. This is in case you need to go into Active Directory Restore mode or boot the domain controller into safe mode. Rmember, domain controllers do not have any local accounts.

You will see some warnings. The DNS delegation one is nothing to worry about. The static IP one, however, is something we should talk about. You really want your AD and DNS servers to have static IPs. In fact, DNS can’t function (for long) without it.

When the server comes up, you’ll notice that you are now signing in as HOME\Administrator. Login and we will change the IP address. I’m going to assume you know what to set you IP address to, but here is how:

New-NetIPAddress -IPAddress 192.168.2.110 -DefaultGateway 192.168.2.1 -PrefixLength 24 -InterfaceAlias Ethernet

You can get the interface alias by running Get-NetAdapter. The standard home network is going to be on a 255.255.255.0 subnet mask, which is the equivalent of a /24 network prefix length. If you don’t understand this, you need to study up on subnetting. It will be on the exam.

Now, we want to set the DNS servers on the server. Let’s set one to the server itself (it’s now a DNS server, after all), and set the other to our router.

Set-DnsClientServerAddress -InterfaceAlias Ethernet -ServerAddresses {192.168.2.110,192.168.2.1}

A good ole ipconfig /all can confirm our changes.

You could have changed all this through sconfig, but you don’t need a tutorial for that! The IP can also be set using netsh interface IPv4.

Add a Second DC

Let’s add a second DC. From what we’ve learned, go ahead and take these steps:

  • Rename the server
  • Enable Remote Desktop
  • Set the time zone
  • Set a static IP (I’m setting mine to 192.168.2.111)
  • Set the DNS servers, pointing one of them to DC1
  • Install the Active Directory Domain Services feature.
  • Restart

Don’t use sconfig! (except for remote desktop). You’ll have lots of chances to use it later.

There is a reason that we set one of the DNS server to point to DC1. When you attempt to join a domain, the server will do a DNS lookup for the domain name (Home.local). You router will not have an entry for it, but DC1 does.

Now, all that’s left is to promote it as a domain controller.

Install-ADDSDomainController -DomainName Home.local -InstallDNS:$true

The server will restart, and you will have two functioning domain controllers! If you want to check on your domain controllers, run some of these commands.

# List DCs
netdom query dc

# Check FSMO roles
Get-ADDomain | Select-Object InfrastructureMaster, RIDMaster, PDCEmulator, DomainNamingMaster, SchemaMaster

That’s it for today. Next time, we are going to setup a utility server with (GASP) a GUI and all of the management tools. Note: It’s important to know how to go from Core to Minimal to Full GUI installations.

MCSA 70-10 Lab #4

item.title: MCSA 70-410 Lab #2 - Creating Child VMs
item.description:
Today, we’re going to start creating some child VMs! Last time, we did the hard work of creating a parent VHD. This time, we’re going to create a child VM and put it all in a script so that it only takes a few seconds to spin up another VM.

First, a note. I moved my Parent.vhdx to the root of my lab folder to keep it separate from all of the VMs we’re going to make. It looks like this:

PS E:\> tree /F
Folder PATH listing for volume SSD
Volume serial number is 20B9-6EE9
E:.
└───Lab
    │   Parent.vhdx
    │
    ├───iso
    │       2012R2.ISO
    │       Windows10Pro64.iso
    │       Windows7Pro64SP1.iso
    │
    ├───scripts
    │       Create-Lab.ps1
    │       New-ChildVM.ps1
    │
    └───VM

Without further ado, let’s get started.

Makin’ a Baby Server

Let’s create the first one manually. Just to review, we’ll simply create a new VM with a Child differencing disk, boot it up, and we’re done. Let’s call the new VM Test1 for now.

$vm = New-VM -Name "Test1" -Generation 2
# Save the VM object in $vm for later use

Now, let’s go ahead and attach the network adapter to our “Wired” network.

$vm | Get-VMNetworkAdapter | Connect-VMNetworkAdapter -SwitchName "Wired"

Here’s the fun part: differencing disks.

New-VHD -Path "E:\Lab\VM\Test1.vhdx" -SizeBytes 40gb -ParentPath "E:\Lab\Parent.vhdx" -Differencing
# Notice the -Differencing switch.

$vm | Add-VMHardDiskDrive -Path "E:\Lab\VM\Test1.vhdx"

And finally, let’s set the VHD as the default startup item. This avoids the wait for PXE boot.

$hdd = $vm | Get-VMHardDiskDrive
$vm | Set-VMFirmware -FirstBootDevice $hdd

Start it up!

$vm | Start-VM

It’s going to boot up and look like this:

Look at how easy that is! Install time of < 30 seconds. Great! Now get rid of it.

$vm | Stop-VM
$vm | Remove-VM
Remove-Item E:\Lab\VM\Test1.vhdx

Like all things you’re going to do over and over again, this should be stored as a script. Here’s mine. Customize it or make your own to your liking.

# New-ChildVM.ps1

Param(
  [string]$vmname
)

if(!$vmname){$vmname= Read-Host "New VM Name"}
$vmpath="E:\Lab\VM"
$parentVhd = "E:\Lab\Parent.vhdx"

Write-Host "Creating VM $vmname"
$vm = New-VM -Name $vmname -Generation 2

Write-Host "Connecting Network adapter to VMSwitch"
$vm | Get-VMNetworkAdapter | Connect-VMNetworkAdapter -SwitchName "Wired"

Write-Host "Creating Child Differencing Disk and adding to VM"
New-VHD -Path "$vmpath\$vmname.vhdx" -SizeBytes 40gb -ParentPath $parentVhd -Differencing
$vm | Add-VMHardDiskDrive -Path "$vmpath\$vmname.vhdx"

Write-Host "Setting as Boot Device"
$hdd = $vm | Get-VMHardDiskDrive
$vm | Set-VMFirmware -FirstBootDevice $hdd


Write-Host "Starting $vmname"
$vm | Start-VM

Write-Host "Enjoy your new VM"

Mine’s saved as New-ChildVM.ps1. All I have to do it is CD to the directory and run it. Remember, in PowerShell, you can only execute something in the current directory if it’s prefixed with .\ (which stands for the current directory).

cd E:\Lab\scripts\
.\New-ChildVM.ps1 "Test2"

And if you get something like this…

PS E:\Lab\scripts> .\New-ChildVM.ps1 "Test2"
.\New-ChildVM.ps1 : File E:\Lab\scripts\New-ChildVM.ps1 cannot be loaded because running scripts is disabled on this
system. For more information, see about_Execution_Policies at http://go.microsoft.com/fwlink/?LinkID=135170.
At line:1 char:1
+ .\New-ChildVM.ps1 "Test2"
+ ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
    + CategoryInfo          : SecurityError: (:) [], PSSecurityException
    + FullyQualifiedErrorId : UnauthorizedAccess

You have to run this… 

Set-ExecutionPolicy Unrestricted

And you can try again now. 

.\New-ChildVM.ps1 "Test2"

Once you get everything going to your liking, you’re done for this lesson! Next time, we’re going to start up our first Forest and Domain. You can also get rid of this VM.

MCSA 70-10 Lab #3

item.title: MCSA 70-410 Lab #1 - Setting up the Lab
item.description:
The Technology

Since we’re going to want to run a bunch of servers at once, we need to go ahead and streamline the process. The general process is as follows:

  • Create new VM
  • Install Server 2012 R2
  • Sysprep and shut down
  • Detach VHD, mark read only, use as parent disk
  • Create new child disks for infinite servers

That’s right, we’re going to use differencing disks. Differencing disks let you take a VHD to a certain state and then create child VHDs. It looks just like a normal disk to the guest OS, but changes are only written to the child disk. Let’s break down some numbers:

Traditional installs:

  • Fresh install of Server 2012 R2 Core: 7 GB
  • 10 VMs @ 7 GB each: 70 GB

Differencing Disks:

  • Parent differencing disk: 7GB
  • Child differencing disk: 0GB
  • 10 VMs @ 0 GB each: 7 GB
  • 100 VMs @ 0 GBs each: 7 GB
  • 1000 VMs @ 0 GBs each: 7 GB

You only have to store the changes. I don’t know if this is normally recommended in a production environment, but it is invaluable for running a lab. And here’s the other great side effect: each new installation of Windows only takes as long as creating the VM! Saves time and space!

File structure

This is how I have mine setup. Setup yours however you want.

E:.
└───Lab
    ├───iso
    │       2012R2.ISO
    │       Windows10Pro64.iso
    │       Windows7Pro64SP1.iso
    │
    ├───scripts
    │	      [Scripts I Saved]
    │       1_NewDomain.ps1
    │       Create-Lab.ps1
    │       New-ChildVM.ps1
    │       New-ParentVM.ps1
    │
    └───VM
            [hard drives].vhdx

Create Your First VM

Remember how I said you need to know PowerShell? You shouldn’t need to touch the GUI for anything, except to check yourself. If you need to find a command, check Microsoft’s list of Hyper-V Commands.

Open up an administrative powershell.

Create your first VM with a disk:

New-VM -name "Parent" -NewVHDPath "E:\Lab\VM\Parent.vhdx" -NewVHDSizeBytes 40GB -Generation 2

Name   State CPUUsage(%) MemoryAssigned(M) Uptime   Status             Version
----   ----- ----------- ----------------- ------   ------             -------
Parent Off   0           0                 00:00:00 Operating normally 6.2

Great! You have a vm. Now let’s save the VM object itself (not the name) as a variable. This just makes life easier.

$vm = Get-VM "Parent"

Now you can do cool things like:

$vm | Get-VM

Anyways, now we need to add the ISO file.

$vm | Add-VMDvdDrive -Path "E:\Lab\iso\2012R2.ISO"

And let’s go ahead and set it as the default boot device.

$dvd= $vm | Get-VMDvdDrive
$vm | Set-VMFirmware -FirstBootDevice $dvd

Let’s crank her up!

$vm | Start-VM
$vm.state
Running

Great! It’s running!

So I may have mislead you earlier. Go ahead and open Hyper-V, and double click on your “Parent” machine. It may say “No operating system was loaded. Press a key to retry the boot sequence…” Sorry. Press a key, and then when it says “Press any key to boot from CD”, do that.

Now that it’s up and running, let’s go ahead and set the default boot device back to the VHD. You don’t have to, but we’re all about learning today.

$hdd = $vm | Get-VMHardDiskDrive
$vm | Set-VMFirmware -FirstBootDevice $hd

Creating the Golden VHD

Go ahead and walk through the Server 2012 R2 Core install like normal. Might as well do Datacenter. Once it boots up and asks you to sign in, go ahead and do that.

Once again, Core. We really want to use the Core install for our Parent disk. You can add the GUI features later.

At this point, don’t worry about setting up anything specific, like the name, timezone, etc. We’re going to sysprep it soon.

You may or may not want to install updates (I didn’t my first time around, but I’m doing it now). You’ve probably noticed by now, though, that we have no network connection.

$vm | Get-VMNetworkAdapter

Name            IsManagementOs VMName SwitchName MacAddress   Status              IPAddresses
----            -------------- ------ ---------- ----------   ------              -----------
Network Adapter False          Parent            00155D020A00 {LostCommunication} {}

That’s because we haven’t created a virtual switch yet!

First, let’s find which network adapter we want to use. I only have the one:

Get-NetAdapter

Name                      InterfaceDescription                    ifIndex Status       MacAddress            LinkSpeed ----                      --------------------                    ------- ------       ----------            --------- Ethernet                  Intel(R) Ethernet Connection I217-V           5 Up           D0-50-99-01-B0-23        1 Gbps

We’re going to create a new switch named “Wired”, attach it to the network interface named “Ethernet”, and allow the management OS to share the network adapter (otherwise we would need separate adapters for Hyper-V and the base OS).

New-VMSwitch -Name "Wired" -NetAdapterName "Ethernet" -AllowManagementOS $true

Warning: this switched you to a new network adapter, so you will need to reset any custom IP setting you have made.

Now we can connect our VM to the new switch.

$vm | Get-VMNetworkAdapter | Connect-VMNetworkAdapter -SwitchName "Wired"

You should now be able to ping the outside world from your VM. If you want to run Windows updates, now is the best time to do it.

  • Run sconfig
  • Choose option 6
  • Press “A” for all updates or “R” for Recommended updates (I chose “R”)
  • Wait
  • Press “A” for all updates
  • Wait more
  • Restart when Prompted

Becoming a Parent

Go ahead and log back in to your VM. We are going to Sysprep the machine, which resets the GUID and gets it ready to be “Unboxed” again. You would do this before you, for instance, sold a machine.

cd C:\Windows\System32\Sysprep
sysprep.exe

Make the setting match the image below and hit “OK”.

If you go look at your Parent.vhdx, it should be somewhere in the neighborhood of 8GB. Let’s see if we can compact it.

Mount-VHD "E:\Lab\VM\Parent.vhdx" -ReadOnly
Optimize-VHD "E:\Lab\VM\Parent.vhdx" -Mode full
Dismount-VHD "E:\Lab\VM\Parent.vhdx"

I saved myself 400MB. Yay.

Now we’re ready to truly make this a parent disk. First of all, we don’t need that virtual machine anymore. Go ahead and get rid of it.

 $vm | Remove-VM

We don’t want to Parent.vhdx disk to ever change because that will invalidate all of the child disks. Let’s mark it as Read Only.

Set-ItemProperty -Path "E:\Lab\VM\Parent.vhdx" -Name IsReadOnly -Value $true

If you go to the file’s properties, you’ll see that it’s marked as Read Only.

Ta-Da!!

We’re all done for today. In the next post, we’ll make a simple script to bring up new VMs in a single command!

MCSA 70-10 Lab #2

item.title: MCSA 70-410 Lab #0 - Intro
item.description:
Today we will look at setting up a lab to study for the MCSA 70-410 exam. This lab is in no way meant to replace other methods of study, like books and training videos, but to supplement them. The first time I took the 70-410 exam, the PowerShell kicked my butt. Now, I know how to use PowerShell. I really enjoy automating day-to-day tasks in PowerShell. However, I did not know all of the commands associated with Active Directory, Windows Features, Group Policy, Firewall, Storage, DNS, DHCP, etc., as well as I thought.

It turns out, just reading books and watching videos didn’t quite do it for me. That is the purpose of this lab: to do everything we can think of that could possibly be covered in this exam in PowerShell.

Caveat: I have not passed the 70-410 yet. I’m writing this as I’m creating this lab and studying. Let’s begin.

Requirements:

  • Windows 8.1 or 10 Pro (This is so that you can run Hyper-V)
  • At least 30 GB of SSD space (You can use a hard drive, but it’s going to be slooowww)
  • A basic knowledge of Hyper-V and PowerShell
  • Server 2012 R2 iso (you should be able to sign up for a demo - no licenses needed)

If you’re looking for a new SSD, the SanDisk Ultra II is very cost-effective. I transferred my C:\ drive here and used my smaller SSD as a dedicated lab drive.

I’m going to list a lot of complete PowerShell commands, but I strongly suggest you type them out instead of copying them. That’s the only way they’ll stick. In fact, after you start typing a command in PowerShell, like “Get-VM”, type a space, then a dash, and then start hitting the tab key. That will cycle through all of the parameters of the command and you’ll probably learn something very interesting.

MCSA 70-10 Lab #1

item.title: Micorosft Exams
item.description:
I’m on a quest!

For my MCSE.

I learned pretty quickly that I needed to do everything in a lab environment to drill these PowerShell commands into my head. These tutorials are basically a way of making sure I take good notes. Enjoy!

MCSA 70-410

item.title: About Me
item.description:
Hi, I’m Andrew.

I’m a Sysadmin located in Memphis, TN. When I’m not working, I like to spend my time playing Minecraft with my wife, trying to pick back up photography, and studying for my MCSE. I also hang around /r/sysadmin whenever I get the chance.

I started this site as a way to make sure I take good notes while studying. I’m hoping to post anything I create or learn whether it’s in my day-to-day job or a personal project.

If you’re trying to get your MCSE, good luck! Hopefully this site will help you out.
